text:
By using atom-trapping strategy, a new generation of ceria-based catalysts were prepared by intimately anchoring atomically dispersed $\text{Ru}_1\text{O}_5$ onto specific (100) facets of $\text{CeO}_2$. The properties and activities of these prepared catalysts were studied systematically by density functional theory (DFT) calculations and a series of in-situ characterizations. Interestingly, the obtained $\text{Ru}_1/\text{CeO}_2}$ catalyst exhibits an entirely different catalytic behavior comparing with the traditional metal-on-ceria catalysts. Remarkably, it shows high activity for the oxidative coupling of NO, a key reaction in diesel exhaust aftertreatment, which generally needs to use plenty of precious metals at high amounts. More remarkably, single-atom active species of $\text{Ru}_1/\text{CeO}_2}$ is confirmed to possess excellent stability in various operational tests, such as the repeated heat-treating, and wet condition.

The NOx storage performances of Ru1/CeO2 catalyst were investigated in detail by a variety of techniques. Interestingly, Ru1/CeO2 showed high NOx storage capacities regardless of the low Ru loading of 0.05 wt% through the formation of stable Ru–NO complexes and efficient NOx spillover onto the ceria surface. Unlike the well-known RuO2 nanoparticles, the Ru1O5 centers in Ru1/CeO2 were found to be stable at high calcination temperatures up to 750 °C in air or steam. By using in situ DRIFTS, MS, and DFT, it is found that Ru2+ ions in Ru1/CeO2 locate at the surface of small ruthenium oxide clusters and the corresponding NOx storage and oxidation catalytic processes.

We show that, in addition to oxidation, as low as 0.1–0.5 at % Ru is sufficient to achieve high reactivity at low temperatures in the reduction of NO by CO. Using in situ modulation-excitation infrared and XPS techniques, we elucidate the mechanisms of the NO reduction reaction over this low Ru content and establish that our catalyst offers a large excess of essential oxygen vacancies and $\text{Ce}^{3+}$ even at modest Ru loading, confirming the promise of ceria-based single-atom catalysts as highly active and sustainable catalysts for the removal of NO and CO.
